metadata.note: Candidate 12. No Fourier-transform algebra, no explicit sigma(t) formula, and no uncertainty-relation derivation.

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Throw a baseball through empty space and it keeps its shape. Release a tidy quantum wave packet and it spreads wider even when nothing touches it.

props.command: Why does a free quantum wave packet spread even when no force acts on it?
props.segment: Why a Quantum Particle Spreads Out Over Time
output: Localization requires a range of wave numbers.
For a free particle, different wave numbers travel at different group speeds.

beats.narration_text: A localized particle is not one infinite sine wave. It is a superposition: many nearby wave numbers added with carefully chosen phases. At the start they reinforce in one narrow region and cancel almost everywhere else.

If every component advanced at the same speed, that interference envelope would simply slide along unchanged. The packet would behave like the baseball. But free-particle quantum mechanics does not give every component the same speed.

For a nonrelativistic free particle, energy is proportional to k squared. That makes angular frequency proportional to k squared too. The slope of that curve—the group velocity—is proportional to k. Larger-k components move faster; smaller-k components move slower.
The mechanism: different momenta, different speeds.

Watch three representative components. The slow one falls behind, the central one tracks the packet center, and the fast one runs ahead. Their phases no longer line up in the same narrow interval. Constructive interference is smeared across a wider region.

The probability density starts as a tall narrow bell. Later it is shorter and wider. Its total area remains one: probability is conserved. The center keeps moving at the mean group velocity while the width grows on both sides.
The center travels. The uncertainty cloud spreads.

This is dispersion, not diffusion. No environment jostles the particle, no random collisions are required, and the evolution remains perfectly deterministic at the level of the wavefunction. The spreading comes from the curved energy-versus-wave-number relation.

A numerical picture: start with a narrow Gaussian centered near the origin. After one time step its peak has moved right and dropped. After two time steps it has moved farther and broadened again. Each curve encloses the same area, but no single classical trajectory captures the growing width.

Why does a quantum particle spread out over time? Localization requires many momenta. Free-particle energy bends with k squared, so those momenta travel at different speeds and pull the interference envelope apart. No force is needed. Quantum mechanics supplies the dispersion.

Your turn. Ask Claude: animate a free Gaussian wave packet alongside three of its momentum components. Let me change the momentum spread, then show how that changes the rate at which the probability envelope broadens. Take this prompt and run it on your own.

props.command: Animate a free Gaussian wave packet beside three momentum components. Add a momentum-spread control and show how it changes the envelope's spreading rate.
props.segment: Why a Quantum Particle Spreads Out Over Time

beats.narration_text: Why a Quantum Particle Spreads Out Over Time. Liam, in for Bear.

props.title: Why a Quantum Particle Spreads Out Over Time
props.subline: different momenta move at different speeds — the envelope broadens
